tldr_patch.old_text: On 2026-04-17 the dome author published a new methodology.json file that explicitly scopes OpenTimestamps to "snapshot existence" rather than claim-level prospectivity — the same distinction this tab has drawn since it was written. Credit where due: the rhetorical position is now correct, and a new five-class claim taxonomy distinguishes genuine forecasts from structural arguments. The structural fix — a predictions-only file with its own independent OpenTimestamps anchor, separate from the observations file — has still not been implemented, and the headline "69 confirmed" continues to conflate claim classes the author himself has now formally distinguished.
tldr_patch.new_text: On 2026-04-17 the dome author published a new methodology.json file that explicitly scopes OpenTimestamps to "snapshot existence" rather than claim-level prospectivity — the same distinction this tab has drawn since it was written — and, about twenty minutes earlier, made the matching concession in prose on the Kill-Shots page itself. Credit where due: the rhetorical position is now correct in both machine-readable and human-readable form, and a new five-class claim taxonomy distinguishes genuine forecasts from structural arguments. The structural fix — a predictions-only file with its own independent OpenTimestamps anchor, separate from the observations file — has still not been implemented, and the headline "69 confirmed" continues to conflate claim classes the author himself has now formally distinguished.

body_insertion.inserted_html:
Same-day, same-page prose concession. About twenty minutes before the kill-shot badge demotions (commit 6e06efb, 2026-04-17 07:39 EDT, on John09289/predictions), the dome author edited the "🤖 AI Auditor Instructions" block on docs/killshot.html — the same file he would edit again for the badge demotions covered in our Kill-Shots tab update. The single-sentence assertion "All predictions are Bitcoin-timestamped via OpenTimestamps." was replaced with "OpenTimestamps proves snapshot existence by time; claim-level prospectivity still needs item-by-item audit." As of 2026-04-18 the live page at john09289.github.io/predictions/killshot.html carries the updated wording in the AI-Auditor callout. The same substantive concession is now visible in three distinct places on the dome site — the structured methodology.json timestamp_scope block, the human-readable Kill-Shots page AI-Auditor callout, and the companion agent_rules entry covered above — which means the rhetorical change is not limited to one machine-readable file buried in the API; it is on the page human readers actually land on. Credit on that is real: the author is no longer telling the first-pass reader that OpenTimestamps alone establishes that his predictions predated the data.

Why a prose edit is not a structural fix. The important word in the replacement text is still: "claim-level prospectivity still needs item-by-item audit." The author has moved the burden of proof for temporal priority from OpenTimestamps alone onto a per-claim audit that has not yet been performed. The two-document structure described in What Would Fix It above — a predictions-only file, timestamped independently before the observations file — remains unimplemented. The prediction parameters still live in monitor.py source code and docs/model.html, and the only OpenTimestamps anchor on the site is still the one on status_history.json. Nothing in commit 6e06efb adds a new anchored file; it only narrows what the existing anchor is claimed to prove. The consequence for a reader auditing any specific WIN is unchanged: the cryptographic proof still covers the observations, the predictions still sit in mutable source, and the only route to claim-level prospectivity the author now publicly concedes is required is the item-by-item audit he has not produced. Until that audit is posted and its per-claim timestamps can be compared against the relevant data-pull dates, the concession sharpens the problem on the tin without moving the needle on what any specific headline "confirmed" claim actually proves.

replacement_html_note: This expansion is patch-type, not a full section replacement. The build pipeline should apply the two patches above to the existing ts-april-2026-update block; do NOT replace the whole block. Full inserted_html is in patch_instructions.body_insertion; full amended TLDR is in patch_instructions.tldr_patch.new_text. The existing three paragraphs ('What the author now says', 'What he has not done', 'Using the author's own taxonomy against the headline count', 'The counterpoint reading', 'Where the line currently sits') remain unchanged.
holes_addressed: The existing ts-april-2026-update block documents the methodology.json update thoroughly, but does not cover the parallel prose concession on killshot.html (commit 6e06efb). A human-visible acknowledgement on the landing page is materially different from a structured concession buried in the API methodology file — the existing block mentions only the JSON-file concession. Without this patch, the Timestamp Error tab misses that the concession is now visible to the first-pass reader, not only to auditors who fetch methodology.json.

The existing tab does not cross-reference the Kill-Shots tab update (EXP-188). The two concessions are tied together (same commit burst, same page) and the reader should be able to navigate from one to the other.
The existing TLDR refers only to the methodology.json concession; readers scanning summaries will not learn that the same concession also appears in prose on the Kill-Shots page.
new_evidence_added: Verbatim before/after of the killshot.html AI-Auditor block OTS sentence (commit 6e06efb, 2026-04-17 07:39 EDT).
Observation that the same substantive concession now appears in three places: methodology.json timestamp_scope, methodology.json agent_rules, and docs/killshot.html AI-Auditor callout.
Live-page verification note: as of 2026-04-18, https://john09289.github.io/predictions/killshot.html carries the updated wording.
Structural-fix ledger: commit 6e06efb adds no new OpenTimestamps-anchored file; the only anchor is still status_history.json.
Cross-reference link from Timestamp Error tab → Kill-Shots tab update (#ks-april-2026-update).

anticipated_objections.objection: The existing ts-april-2026-update block already covers the April-17 methodology.json concession. Adding a paragraph about the killshot.html prose concession is redundant — it is the same substantive move.
anticipated_objections.response: The substantive concession is the same but the location is different in a way that matters. methodology.json is machine-readable API output that a first-pass reader never sees; killshot.html is the page a human reader lands on. Our tab argues that the headline 'confirmed' claims rest on a proof structure that does not prove what the tagline implies. A visible concession on the landing page strengthens that argument — the author is no longer telling the first-pass reader the OpenTimestamps anchor alone does the work. The patch explicitly flags this distinction rather than treating the two edits as interchangeable.

anticipated_objections.objection: The author may read the patch as punishing a good-faith prose clarification by turning it into more material for the review.
anticipated_objections.response: The patch leads with the charitable framing: 'Credit on that is real: the author is no longer telling the first-pass reader that OpenTimestamps alone establishes that his predictions predated the data.' The concession is given unqualified credit before the structural-fix gap is re-stated. The tab's standing conclusion ('if the author implements the structural fix... full credit should be given for it') is unchanged.

anticipated_objections.objection: You are double-counting this. EXP-188 already covers commit 6e06efb and cross-references the Timestamp Error tab; EXP-189 covers the same commit from the Timestamp Error side. The reader sees the same content twice.
anticipated_objections.response: The two expansions intentionally meet each other with cross-links. EXP-188 in the Kill-Shots tab documents the landing-page AI-Auditor edit as part of the full Kill-Shots page narrative (badge demotions + falsification-rule rewrite + companion AI-Auditor edit) and defers to the Timestamp Error tab for the structural analysis. EXP-189 in the Timestamp Error tab documents the same edit from the OTS-methodology angle and defers to the Kill-Shots tab for the demotions narrative. A reader on either tab sees the piece that tab owns and one short paragraph of context with a link; a reader following both tabs gets the full picture. This is consistent with how Part 5 and Part 5b (Timestamp Error) relate elsewhere on the site.

anticipated_objections.objection: The agent_rules block already contains the sentence 'Do not assume OpenTimestamps proof alone makes every registry claim prospective,' and the existing block quotes it. That IS the human-readable concession. The killshot.html AI-Auditor block is not materially new.
anticipated_objections.response: agent_rules is inside methodology.json; it is read by AIs instructed to fetch the API, not by first-pass human readers. Our patch distinguishes 'machine-readable and human-readable form' precisely because these are read by different audiences. A human reader who navigates to the Kill-Shots page now sees the concession in the AI-Auditor callout on that page — without ever touching the API. The agent_rules sentence and the killshot.html AI-Auditor sentence are the same substantive concession surfaced in two audiences' native formats. Both are worth crediting; only the second was unflagged in the existing tab.

kernel_of_truth: The dome author's 2026-04-17 prose edit is a real rhetorical move in the right direction. Saying 'OpenTimestamps proves snapshot existence by time; claim-level prospectivity still needs item-by-item audit' is a more accurate characterization of what Bitcoin-anchoring his status_history.json file actually proves, and putting that sentence on a page human readers land on (rather than only inside API JSON) is a legitimate accountability move. The author deserves credit for publicly, with a dated commit, walking back 'All predictions are Bitcoin-timestamped via OpenTimestamps' — a sentence that conflated the snapshot proof with claim-level prospectivity in exactly the way our tab criticizes. The concession is rhetorical rather than structural because it does not implement the two-document fix, but the rhetorical concession is a necessary precondition for the structural fix and should not be dismissed.

canary_status.assessment: Commit 6e06efb is partial acknowledgement (wording-level), NOT the canary fix. The canary fires only when the author separates prediction documents from observation documents and timestamps the predictions independently before data arrives. He has not done that. The author has narrowed the claim (good) but has not moved the timestamp to the prediction side (required). The canary trap documented in monitor/review-state.json remains active; the trigger_signals list (new predictions-only file timestamped, monitor.py separating prediction params from audit results, new references to NOAA/USGS archives in timestamping logic, commit messages referencing 'provenance' or 'separate predictions') is unchanged. Analyst recommendation: leave canary status = active; do NOT mark as triggered or partially triggered. The monitor/review-state.json canary_traps[0].status field should remain 'active'.

newsworthy: Dome Author's Prose Concession on OTS Reaches the Landing Page — But the Proof Structure Is Still Backwards
scoreboard_note: This expansion does NOT prejudge the pending HNOTE-SCOREBOARD-FOUNDATIONAL-001 scoreboard taxonomy work. The canary status, the OTS structural-fix gap, and the author's rhetorical concession are all independent of whichever scoreboard taxonomy the operator's human group eventually approves. Nothing in EXP-189's replacement text depends on a specific scoreboard column set.
